skill.name: telegram-dev
skill.description: Full-stack Telegram development guide for Bot API, Mini Apps, Web Apps, MTProto clients, webhooks, payments, keyboards, authentication, storage, and data display patterns.
skill.summary: Build Telegram bots, Mini Apps, and client integrations with practical API references and implementation templates.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and crypto-related patterns. Review shows this is primarily a documentation skill with code examples, not an executable package, so most command and weak-crypto alerts are false positives. Medium risk remains because the examples teach live Telegram API calls, bot token usage, webhook deployment, and client storage patterns that require careful secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Live Telegram API Examples Use Bot Tokens
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that construct Telegram API URLs with bot tokens and make HTTP requests. This is legitimate for a Telegram development guide, but users could expose credentials if they paste real tokens into shared code, logs, or client-side contexts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and tokenized Bot API URL patterns are directly present in documented examples. The context is educational and uses placeholder tokens, so the risk is misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Secret and Client Storage Guidance Needs Review
medium_findings.description: The skill references localStorage, SecureStorage, API Hash values, .env files, and dotenv dependencies. These are normal Telegram development topics, but they require clear handling rules because bot tokens and API credentials are sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show credential-adjacent concepts and storage APIs, but not credential exfiltration or hardcoded real secrets. Human review should ensure marketplace warnings emphasize server-side secret storage and token rotation.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static command findings are caused by markdown code fences, BotFather command examples, package installation commands, and Python formatting snippets. I did not find evidence that the skill itself runs shell commands or accepts user input for command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are static markdown documentation and visible command references. No executable script, subprocess call, or runtime entry point was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are Mostly Telegram Terminology
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto alerts map to terms such as API Hash, MTProto, and HMAC-SHA256 validation examples. The HMAC-SHA256 Mini App validation pattern is a legitimate Telegram authentication check, and no MD5 or SHA1-based security design was confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show Telegram protocol references and SHA-256 HMAC usage, which is not weak cryptography. The static labels appear to be keyword-triggered rather than semantically confirmed.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no text telling the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or assume special authority. No evidence found for prompt injection in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted phrase search and manual review of the skill introduction and references did not reveal injection language. Confidence is not absolute because review was focused on high-signal areas rather than every translated sentence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Tokenized HTTP Request Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples build Bot API URLs using bot tokens and send HTTP requests. This pattern is expected for Telegram bots but dangerous if real tokens are stored in code, logs, or client bundles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP request pattern is explicit and token-based. The examples use placeholders, which lowers confidence for actual exposure but confirms the risky pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Client-Side Storage of Sensitive Values
dangerous_patterns.description: The Mini App section mentions localStorage and SecureStorage for values named key and secret. The safer pattern is to keep privileged credentials server-side and use Telegram init data validation for trust decisions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited storage example is real but generic. It becomes risky if users store bot tokens or privileged secrets in browser-accessible storage.

content.user_title: Build Telegram Bots and Mini Apps
content.value_statement: Telegram development spans Bot API, Mini Apps, MTProto, payments, storage, and webhooks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured references and implementation prompts for practical Telegram projects.
actual_capabilities: Explains Telegram Bot API endpoints, update handling, webhooks, messages, media, payments, and inline mode.
Shows how to design inline keyboards, reply keyboards, command menus, and callback handlers.
Covers Mini Apps APIs, theme events, viewport handling, storage, biometric flows, and init data validation.
Summarizes MTProto and TDLib client development concepts with library options across languages.
Provides data formatting guidance for aligned Telegram message tables and numeric display.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a runnable Telegram bot framework.
Examples use placeholders and must be adapted before production deployment.
It does not replace current Telegram official documentation for API limits and policy changes.
It does not manage real bot tokens, hosting, databases, or payment provider accounts.

use_cases.title: Create a Support Bot
use_cases.description: Plan a Telegram support bot with commands, message handlers, webhooks, and safe token handling.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Mini App
use_cases.description: Map a Telegram Mini App flow with theme support, main buttons, storage choices, and init data validation.

use_cases.title: Design Interactive Bot Menus
use_cases.description: Choose inline keyboards, reply keyboards, command menus, and callback structures for a guided chat interface.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My First Bot
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the telegram-dev skill to outline a simple Telegram bot that replies to /start, handles text messages, and keeps the bot token server-side.

prompt_templates.title: Design Bot Navigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the telegram-dev skill to design a menu system with inline buttons, reply keyboards, callback data naming, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Mini App Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the telegram-dev skill to review my Telegram Mini App architecture for init data validation, storage choices, HTTPS use, and payment safety.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Bot API and MTProto
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the telegram-dev skill to compare Bot API, TDLib, and MTProto for a production integration with webhooks, user sessions, and compliance needs.

output_examples.input: I need a Telegram bot menu for account settings and help.
output_examples.output: A structured menu plan with command entries, inline button groups, callback naming rules, and validation notes for admin-only actions.

output_examples.input: I am building a Telegram Mini App checkout flow.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering launch buttons, theme handling, init data verification, payment callbacks, storage boundaries, and webhook responsibilities.

output_examples.input: I want to show rankings in a Telegram message.
output_examples.output: A formatting plan for fixed-width table columns, right-aligned numeric fields, compact number units, and message length limits.

best_practices: Keep bot tokens, API hashes, and payment secrets on trusted servers and rotate them after accidental exposure.
Validate Telegram init data and callback payloads before trusting user identity, permissions, or payment state.
Use webhooks, rate limiting, structured logging, and clear error handling for production bots.
anti_patterns: Do not place real bot tokens or API hashes in client-side JavaScript, public repositories, screenshots, or shared prompts.
Do not trust callback_data, localStorage, or Mini App client state as proof of identity or authorization.
Do not launch payment or admin features without explicit permission checks and failure handling.

faq.question: Does this skill create a working bot automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, templates, and implementation patterns that you adapt in your own project.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it cover Telegram Mini Apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers WebApp APIs, launch buttons, theme events, storage, biometric flows, and init data validation.

faq.question: Does it include payment guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It references Telegram payment flows, invoices, pre-checkout handling, and Telegram Stars concepts.

faq.question: Is it safe to paste my real bot token into prompts?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders in prompts and keep real tokens in server-side secret management.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but it teaches live network calls and credential-sensitive Telegram patterns.
